options.text: Injection therapy to reduce knee inflammation and pain
options.explanation: Injections may relieve symptoms but do not replace the damaged knee joint.

options.text: Arthroscopic cleaning of torn cartilage without joint replacement
options.explanation: Arthroscopy may treat some knee problems, but it does not involve replacing the joint surfaces.

options.text: Physical therapy to strengthen muscles around the knee
options.explanation: Physical therapy can help function, but it is not a surgical joint replacement.

question: What is a cystocele?

options.text: Bulging of the bladder into the vagina
options.explanation: A cystocele is a prolapsed bladder that bulges into the vagina.

options.text: Prolapse of the uterus into the vagina
options.explanation: That describes a uterine prolapse, not a cystocele.

options.text: Herniation of the rectum into the vagina
options.explanation: That describes a rectocele, not a cystocele.

options.text: Inflammation of the bladder lining
options.explanation: That describes cystitis, not pelvic organ prolapse.

question: What best describes macrodactyly of the hand?

options.text: Congenital overgrowth of fingers from bone and soft tissue enlargement
options.explanation: Macrodactyly is a rare congenital condition causing abnormal enlargement of one or more fingers from overgrowth of bone and soft tissue.

options.text: Acquired shortening of fingers due to tendon contractures
options.explanation: This describes contracture-related deformity, not congenital digital overgrowth.

options.text: Inflammatory swelling of the hand from infection
options.explanation: Infection can cause swelling, but it does not produce the characteristic lifelong finger overgrowth.

options.text: Congenital fusion of all fingers into one digit
options.explanation: Fusion of digits is syndactyly, not macrodactyly.

question: Which statement best describes circumcision in newborn boys?

options.text: Removal of the foreskin with potential medical benefits and small risks
options.explanation: Circumcision is the removal of the foreskin and has possible benefits with low risks.

options.text: Removal of the glans penis to prevent urinary infections
options.explanation: Circumcision does not remove the glans penis; it removes the foreskin.

options.text: Surgical repair of a penile curvature in infancy
options.explanation: Penile curvature repair is a different surgery and not circumcision.

options.text: Routine removal of the testes to reduce cancer risk
options.explanation: Removing the testes is orchiectomy, not circumcision.

question: How is Mondini dysplasia diagnosed in many cases?

options.text: By clinical imaging and associated syndromic evaluation
options.explanation: Mondini dysplasia is typically diagnosed by imaging, and genetic testing is only available in some syndromic or familial cases.

options.text: By routine blood culture testing
options.explanation: Blood cultures do not evaluate inner ear malformations or genetic syndromes.

options.text: By lumbar puncture and CSF analysis
options.explanation: CSF analysis is not used to diagnose congenital cochlear malformations.

options.text: By skin biopsy for enzyme levels
options.explanation: Skin biopsy for enzyme levels is not a standard diagnostic test for Mondini dysplasia.

question: What is an aberrant subclavian artery?

options.text: A congenital vascular anomaly often found incidentally
options.explanation: Aberrant subclavian artery is a rare congenital vascular anomaly often discovered incidentally and usually asymptomatic.

options.text: An acquired narrowing of the carotid artery
options.explanation: It is not an acquired carotid narrowing; it is a congenital anomaly of the subclavian artery.

options.text: A congenital heart valve defect causing cyanosis
options.explanation: It is not a valve defect and does not primarily cause cyanosis.

options.text: An inflammatory aneurysm of the aorta
options.explanation: It is not an inflammatory aortic aneurysm, though it may compress the esophagus and cause dysphagia.

question: What is isolated levocardia?

options.text: Heart in normal position with mirror-image reversal of other organs
options.explanation: Isolated levocardia means the heart remains in the normal left-sided position while other viscera are mirror-imaged.

options.text: Heart and abdominal organs both reversed
options.explanation: That describes complete situs inversus, not isolated levocardia.

options.text: Heart displaced to the right side only
options.explanation: Right-sided heart position is dextrocardia, not isolated levocardia.

options.text: Normal organ arrangement with dextrocardia
options.explanation: Normal organ arrangement with dextrocardia is not the definition of isolated levocardia.

question: Which statement best describes ankle injuries and disorders?

options.text: They mainly involve sprains and fractures of the ankle joint.
options.explanation: Ankle injuries commonly include sprains and fractures involving the joint and surrounding structures.

options.text: They are usually infections of the ankle bones and skin.
options.explanation: Infections can affect the ankle, but they are not the most common ankle injuries and disorders.

options.text: They only affect the Achilles tendon and calf muscles.
options.explanation: Ankle problems can involve tendons, but they are not limited to the Achilles tendon and calf muscles.

options.text: They are caused only by arthritis in older adults.
options.explanation: Arthritis can affect the ankle, but ankle injuries are not caused only by arthritis in older adults.

question: Which statement best describes elbow injuries and disorders?

options.text: Problems affecting elbow bones, cartilage, ligaments, tendons, muscles, or fluid
options.explanation: Elbow injuries and disorders can involve any of the joint's structures, including bone, cartilage, ligaments, tendons, muscles, and fluid.

options.text: Only fractures and dislocations of the elbow joint
options.explanation: Elbow disorders are not limited to fractures and dislocations; many soft tissue problems can also cause pain.

options.text: Inflammation limited to the elbow bursa
options.explanation: Bursitis is only one possible elbow disorder, not the full definition of elbow injuries and disorders.

options.text: Pain caused only by arthritis in older adults
options.explanation: Arthritis is only one cause of elbow pain, and elbow problems can occur at any age.

question: Which statement best describes facial injuries and disorders?

options.text: They only affect facial appearance and never function.
options.explanation: Facial conditions can significantly affect function, not just appearance.

options.text: They can cause pain and impair sight, speech, breathing, or swallowing.
options.explanation: Facial injuries and disorders can cause pain and affect appearance, as well as vital functions like sight, speech, breathing, and swallowing.

options.text: They are limited to skin infections of the face.
options.explanation: They include fractures, nerve disorders, and birth defects, not only skin infections.

options.text: They only occur after dental procedures.
options.explanation: They can result from trauma, disease, or birth defects, not just dental procedures.

question: What is a urachal cyst?

options.text: A sac-like pocket in a persistent remnant of the urachus
options.explanation: A urachal cyst forms from a remnant of the urachus, the embryologic connection between the umbilicus and bladder.

options.text: A congenital cyst arising from the renal cortex
options.explanation: Renal cortical cysts arise in the kidney, not from the urachus.

options.text: A fluid-filled dilation of the ureter
options.explanation: Ureteral dilation is a different condition and does not describe a urachal cyst.

options.text: An abscess within the urinary bladder wall
options.explanation: A bladder wall abscess is an infection, not a congenital urachal remnant.

question: Which treatment is most appropriate for Mondini dysplasia to prevent recurrent meningitis and address hearing loss?

options.text: Surgical repair, prophylactic antibiotics, pneumococcal vaccination, and hearing support
options.explanation: Mondini dysplasia is treated with defect repair to prevent meningitis, plus infection prevention and hearing rehabilitation.

options.text: High-dose corticosteroids alone
options.explanation: Corticosteroids do not correct the structural defect or prevent recurrent meningitis.

options.text: Observation without intervention
options.explanation: Observation alone leaves the defect and infection risk untreated.

options.text: Antiviral therapy and tympanostomy tubes
options.explanation: Antivirals do not treat this congenital inner ear malformation, and tympanostomy tubes are not definitive therapy.

question: What is the main goal of ergonomics in the workplace?

options.text: To maximize productivity regardless of worker comfort
options.explanation: Productivity alone is not the main goal if it increases strain or injury.

options.text: To fit job conditions to the worker for safety and comfort
options.explanation: Ergonomics aims to match work, tools, and environment to the worker to reduce injury and improve comfort.

options.text: To replace all manual tasks with automation
options.explanation: Ergonomics does not require eliminating manual tasks, only optimizing them.

options.text: To increase workplace noise and stimulation
options.explanation: Ergonomics seeks to reduce harmful exposures, not increase them.

question: What is the most likely cause of Chiari malformation type 1?

options.text: Congenital structural defect during fetal development
options.explanation: Chiari malformation type 1 is usually due to a congenital structural defect developing during fetal life.

options.text: Autoimmune destruction of cerebellar tissue
options.explanation: Autoimmune cerebellar destruction does not cause the characteristic hindbrain herniation of Chiari I.

options.text: Bacterial infection of the meninges in adulthood
options.explanation: Meningitis may be associated with acquired causes, but it is not the primary typical cause of Chiari I.

options.text: Ischemic stroke of the posterior fossa
options.explanation: Posterior fossa stroke is not the underlying developmental mechanism of Chiari malformation type 1.

question: Which statement best describes leg injuries and disorders?

options.text: They only involve bones and never affect joints or vessels.
options.explanation: Leg problems can affect muscles, joints, and blood vessels, not just bones.

options.text: They include sprains, fractures, dislocations, and diseases like osteoarthritis or DVT.
options.explanation: Leg injuries and disorders can involve trauma to bones, joints, muscles, and vessels, as well as diseases like osteoarthritis and deep vein thrombosis.

options.text: They are limited to sports injuries of the ankle and foot.
options.explanation: Leg injuries can affect the entire leg, including the knee and hip, and may be non-sports related.

options.text: They occur only from infections and never from trauma.
options.explanation: Leg disorders commonly result from trauma and degenerative or vascular disease, not only infection.

question: Which structure is the movable lower part of the jaw used for talking and chewing?

options.text: Maxilla
options.explanation: The maxilla is the upper jaw and does not move.

options.text: Mandible
options.explanation: The mandible is the movable lower jaw that moves during talking and chewing.

options.text: Temporomandibular joint
options.explanation: The temporomandibular joint connects the mandible to the skull, but it is not the jaw bone itself.

options.text: Chin
options.explanation: The chin is the front part where the two halves of the mandible meet, not the whole lower jaw.

question: What embryologic defect causes a laryngeal cleft?

options.text: Failure of the tracheoesophageal septum to form properly
options.explanation: A laryngeal cleft results from failed separation of the foregut by the tracheoesophageal septum.

options.text: Incomplete fusion of the palatal shelves
options.explanation: Incomplete fusion of the palatal shelves causes cleft palate, not laryngeal cleft.

options.text: Persistence of the thyroglossal duct
options.explanation: Persistence of the thyroglossal duct causes a thyroglossal duct cyst, not a laryngeal cleft.

options.text: Abnormal migration of neural crest cells
options.explanation: Abnormal neural crest migration is associated with other congenital syndromes, not this defect.

question: Which of the following best describes eyelid disorders?

options.text: Only infections of the eyelid skin
options.explanation: Eyelid disorders are not limited to infections; they also include structural and neoplastic conditions.

options.text: Problems causing drooping, turning, inflammation, or cancer
options.explanation: Eyelid disorders include infection, inflammation, cancer, and structural or movement problems like drooping or turning.

options.text: Disorders limited to tear production by the lacrimal gland
options.explanation: Tear production problems involve the lacrimal system, not the eyelids themselves.

options.text: Retinal diseases affecting vision only
options.explanation: Retinal diseases affect the back of the eye, not the eyelids.

question: What is a vein of Galen aneurysm?

options.text: A rare arteriovenous malformation causing dilated deep cerebral venous drainage
options.explanation: It is a rare arteriovenous malformation involving dilation of the vein of Galen, often causing high-output heart failure.

options.text: A saccular aneurysm of the middle cerebral artery
options.explanation: A middle cerebral artery aneurysm is an arterial lesion, not a vein of Galen malformation.

options.text: A venous thrombosis causing intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: Venous thrombosis is an acquired clotting disorder, not a congenital arteriovenous malformation.

options.text: A congenital narrowing of the cerebral aqueduct
options.explanation: Aqueductal narrowing can cause hydrocephalus, but it does not describe a vein of Galen aneurysm.

question: Which description best defines hemifacial microsomia?

options.text: Unilateral underdevelopment of facial structures, often with ear and jaw anomalies
options.explanation: Hemifacial microsomia is characterized by underdevelopment of one side of the face, often involving the jaw, ear, and related structures.

options.text: Bilateral absence of facial bones with normal hearing and tongue size
options.explanation: Hemifacial microsomia is typically unilateral, not bilateral, and may include hearing and tongue abnormalities.

options.text: Progressive facial muscle weakness caused by autoimmune inflammation
options.explanation: It is a congenital developmental anomaly, not an acquired inflammatory muscle disease.

options.text: Congenital overgrowth of one side of the face with enlarged jaw
options.explanation: The condition involves undergrowth, not overgrowth, of facial structures.

question: Which management approach is appropriate for enlarged parietal foramina?

options.text: Observation and supportive care
options.explanation: Enlarged parietal foramina are usually managed conservatively with observation and supportive care.

options.text: High-dose antibiotics
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat this congenital skull defect unless there is a separate infection.

options.text: Immediate chemotherapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy has no role in managing a bony developmental anomaly.

options.text: Emergency splenectomy
options.explanation: Splenectomy is unrelated to skull foramina and is not a treatment for this condition.

question: What is the appropriate treatment for isolated situs inversus?

options.text: No treatment is usually necessary
options.explanation: Isolated situs inversus typically requires no treatment unless another associated condition is present.

options.text: Immediate surgical correction of organ position
options.explanation: Surgery is not used to reverse organ position in isolated situs inversus.

options.text: Long-term antibiotic therapy
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat a congenital positional anomaly.

options.text: Radiation therapy to normalize anatomy
options.explanation: Radiation has no role in correcting situs inversus.

question: Which condition is included among penis disorders?

options.text: Erectile dysfunction
options.explanation: Erectile dysfunction is a common penis disorder causing inability to get or keep an erection.

options.text: Appendicitis
options.explanation: Appendicitis is inflammation of the appendix, not a penis disorder.

options.text: Nephrolithiasis
options.explanation: Nephrolithiasis is kidney stone disease and does not involve the penis.

options.text: Otitis media
options.explanation: Otitis media is a middle ear infection, unrelated to penis disorders.

question: Which condition is included among tailbone disorders?

options.text: Tailbone injuries, pain, infections, cysts, and tumors
options.explanation: Tailbone disorders include injuries, pain, infections, cysts, and tumors affecting the coccyx.

options.text: Only vertebral fractures and spinal cord tumors
options.explanation: Tailbone disorders are not limited to vertebral fractures or spinal cord tumors.

options.text: Hip arthritis and pelvic inflammatory disease
options.explanation: Hip arthritis and pelvic inflammatory disease do not specifically involve the tailbone.

options.text: Kidney stones and bladder infections
options.explanation: Kidney stones and bladder infections are urinary tract conditions, not tailbone disorders.

question: What is the main effect of laser eye surgery on vision?

options.text: It reshapes the cornea to change focusing power
options.explanation: Laser eye surgery reshapes the cornea, which changes how the eye focuses light.

options.text: It replaces the lens with an artificial implant
options.explanation: Artificial lens replacement is cataract surgery, not standard laser eye surgery.

options.text: It permanently dilates the pupil
options.explanation: Laser eye surgery does not work by permanently dilating the pupil.

options.text: It strengthens the retina to improve acuity
options.explanation: It does not strengthen the retina; it primarily alters corneal shape.

question: What is a laryngeal cleft?

options.text: An abnormal opening between the larynx and esophagus
options.explanation: A laryngeal cleft is a rare congenital separation defect creating an opening between the larynx and esophagus.

options.text: A congenital narrowing of the trachea
options.explanation: Tracheal narrowing is tracheal stenosis, not a cleft between the larynx and esophagus.

options.text: A complete absence of the epiglottis
options.explanation: Absence of the epiglottis is a different congenital anomaly and does not define a laryngeal cleft.

options.text: An inflammatory swelling of the vocal cords
options.explanation: Inflammatory vocal cord swelling is laryngitis, not a structural cleft.

question: Which statement best describes common foot injuries and disorders?

options.text: They include bunions, corns, plantar warts, and fallen arches.
options.explanation: Common foot disorders include bunions, corns and calluses, plantar warts, and fallen arches.

options.text: They are usually caused only by infections of the ankle.
options.explanation: Foot disorders are not limited to ankle infections and often involve structural or skin problems.

options.text: They affect only athletes and never older adults.
options.explanation: Aging and being overweight also increase foot problems, so they are not limited to athletes.

options.text: They are limited to fractures of the toes and heel.
options.explanation: Foot injuries and disorders include many conditions beyond fractures, such as bunions and flat feet.

question: What best describes a CT scan?

options.text: A painless imaging test using special X-rays to create cross-sectional body pictures
options.explanation: CT uses special X-ray equipment to produce cross-sectional images and is generally painless.

options.text: A blood test measuring organ enzymes and electrolytes
options.explanation: Blood tests do not create images of the body.

options.text: An ultrasound exam using sound waves to view soft tissues
options.explanation: Ultrasound uses sound waves, not X-rays, and is a different imaging modality.

options.text: A magnetic test that uses radioactivity to image bones
options.explanation: MRI uses magnets and radio waves, not radioactivity, and CT is not a magnetic test.

question: What is Brown syndrome?

options.text: Restriction of up-and-in eye movement due to superior oblique tendon dysfunction
options.explanation: Brown syndrome classically causes limited elevation in adduction from superior oblique tendon dysfunction.

options.text: Paralysis of the lateral rectus causing inability to abduct the eye
options.explanation: Lateral rectus paralysis causes abduction weakness, which is not the defining feature of Brown syndrome.

options.text: Inflammation of the optic nerve causing painful vision loss
options.explanation: Optic neuritis affects vision and pain, not the characteristic eye movement restriction of Brown syndrome.

options.text: Retinal detachment causing flashes and floaters
options.explanation: Retinal detachment is a retinal disorder and does not cause the specific motility defect seen in Brown syndrome.

question: Which test is commonly used to help diagnose thoracic outlet syndrome?

options.text: Nerve conduction studies
options.explanation: Nerve conduction studies can help evaluate neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome.

options.text: Serum troponin testing
options.explanation: Troponin testing assesses myocardial injury, not thoracic outlet syndrome.

options.text: Electroencephalography
options.explanation: EEG evaluates brain electrical activity and is not used for thoracic outlet syndrome.

options.text: Colonoscopy
options.explanation: Colonoscopy examines the colon and has no role in diagnosing thoracic outlet syndrome.

question: What causes a thyroglossal tract cyst?

options.text: Persistence of the thyroglossal duct after thyroid descent
options.explanation: A thyroglossal cyst results from persistence of the embryologic thyroglossal duct.

options.text: Obstruction of the parotid duct
options.explanation: Parotid duct obstruction causes a salivary gland problem, not a midline neck cyst.

options.text: Infection of the submandibular gland
options.explanation: Submandibular gland infection causes sialadenitis, not a thyroglossal duct cyst.

options.text: Failure of fusion of the palatal shelves
options.explanation: Failure of palatal shelf fusion causes cleft palate, not a thyroglossal tract cyst.

question: Which statement best describes artificial limbs?

options.text: They are prostheses that can replace a missing arm or leg.
options.explanation: Artificial limbs are prostheses designed to replace a missing arm or leg and help with daily activities.

options.text: They are medications used to improve circulation.
options.explanation: Medications do not replace missing limbs; they may treat underlying circulation problems.

options.text: They are surgical implants that restore nerve function.
options.explanation: Artificial limbs are external devices, not surgical implants that restore nerve function.

options.text: They are braces used only for temporary fractures.
options.explanation: Braces support injured limbs but do not replace an amputated arm or leg.

question: Which management approach is most appropriate for Poland syndrome?

options.text: Surgical correction of chest wall deformities, with breast reconstruction when indicated
options.explanation: Poland syndrome is managed primarily with reconstructive surgery to correct chest wall deformities and improve appearance.

options.text: Immediate chemotherapy to prevent progression of muscle hypoplasia
options.explanation: Chemotherapy has no role in treating a congenital chest wall and muscle malformation.

options.text: Long-term corticosteroids to restore absent pectoral muscles
options.explanation: Corticosteroids cannot regenerate absent pectoral muscles or correct the deformity.

options.text: Antibiotic therapy followed by physical therapy alone
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat this structural congenital condition, and physical therapy alone is insufficient.

question: What is an inguinal hernia?

options.text: A protrusion through the inguinal canal in the lower abdominal wall
options.explanation: An inguinal hernia occurs when tissue protrudes through the inguinal canal in the lower abdominal wall.

options.text: A herniation through the diaphragm into the chest
options.explanation: This describes a hiatal hernia, not an inguinal hernia.

options.text: A bulge through the femoral canal below the inguinal ligament
options.explanation: This describes a femoral hernia, which passes through the femoral canal.

options.text: A defect in the umbilical ring at the navel
options.explanation: This describes an umbilical hernia, which occurs at the navel.

question: What is the most likely cause of Poland syndrome?

options.text: Abnormal vascular disruption during the sixth week of fetal development
options.explanation: Poland syndrome is thought to result from an embryonic vascular disruption, especially involving the subclavian artery, around the sixth week.

options.text: Maternal folate deficiency causing neural tube defects
options.explanation: Folate deficiency causes neural tube defects, not the unilateral chest and limb findings of Poland syndrome.

options.text: Autosomal dominant mutation in collagen synthesis
options.explanation: Collagen synthesis defects cause connective tissue disorders, not the classic vascular disruption seen in Poland syndrome.

options.text: Postnatal trauma to the chest wall
options.explanation: Poland syndrome is congenital and develops in utero, not from trauma after birth.

question: What is knee replacement?

options.text: Replacement of the entire knee joint or only the damaged compartment
options.explanation: Knee replacement can be total, replacing the whole joint, or partial, replacing only the damaged compartment.

options.text: Injection of lubricant into the knee joint
options.explanation: Lubricant injections may relieve symptoms but do not replace the knee joint.

options.text: Removal of the kneecap without joint replacement
options.explanation: Removing the kneecap alone is not a knee replacement procedure.

options.text: Fusion of the knee joint to eliminate movement
options.explanation: Knee fusion is a different operation and does not involve joint replacement.

question: What best describes a perineal injury in males?

options.text: Injury to the area between the anus and scrotum
options.explanation: The male perineum is the area between the anus and the scrotum, so injury there is a perineal injury.

options.text: Injury to the penis only
options.explanation: The penis is not the perineum; perineal injury involves the region behind it.

options.text: Injury to the lower abdomen
options.explanation: The lower abdomen is anatomically separate from the perineum.

options.text: Injury to the buttocks only
options.explanation: The buttocks are posterior gluteal structures, not the perineal region.

question: Which procedure is part of cosmetic dentistry rather than orthodontic treatment?

options.text: Straightening teeth with braces
options.explanation: Braces are orthodontic treatment, not cosmetic dentistry.

options.text: Bleaching teeth to make them whiter
options.explanation: Bleaching is a cosmetic dental procedure used to improve tooth appearance.

options.text: Moving teeth with retainers
options.explanation: Retainers are used in orthodontics to maintain tooth position.

options.text: Correcting jaw alignment surgically
options.explanation: Jaw alignment surgery is not a standard cosmetic dentistry procedure.

question: Which statement best describes the symptoms of isolated situs inversus?

options.text: Usually no symptoms; organs are mirror-imaged
options.explanation: Isolated situs inversus is often asymptomatic and involves a complete mirror-image arrangement of thoracic and abdominal organs.

options.text: Severe cyanosis and clubbing in all patients
options.explanation: Cyanosis and clubbing are not typical of isolated situs inversus and suggest other cardiopulmonary disease.

options.text: Progressive muscle weakness and ptosis
options.explanation: Muscle weakness and ptosis are unrelated to situs inversus and point to neuromuscular disorders.

options.text: Recurrent kidney stones and hematuria
options.explanation: Kidney stones and hematuria are not characteristic features of situs inversus.

question: What is piriformis syndrome?

options.text: Compression of the sciatic nerve by the piriformis muscle
options.explanation: Piriformis syndrome occurs when the piriformis muscle compresses or irritates the sciatic nerve.

options.text: Inflammation of the Achilles tendon causing heel pain
options.explanation: Achilles tendon inflammation causes heel pain, not buttock and sciatic nerve symptoms.

options.text: Entrapment of the median nerve at the wrist
options.explanation: Median nerve entrapment at the wrist is carpal tunnel syndrome, not piriformis syndrome.

options.text: Degeneration of the hip joint cartilage
options.explanation: Hip cartilage degeneration describes osteoarthritis, not sciatic nerve compression.

question: Which advice best helps prevent direct inguinal hernias and reduce recurrence after repair?

options.text: Maintain healthy weight and avoid smoking
options.explanation: Maintaining a healthy weight and not smoking can reduce risk of direct inguinal hernias and recurrence.

options.text: Take daily antibiotics
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not prevent abdominal wall weakness or hernia formation.

options.text: Increase heavy lifting
options.explanation: Heavy lifting increases intra-abdominal pressure and can worsen hernias.

options.text: Drink more milk
options.explanation: Milk intake has no proven role in preventing inguinal hernias.

question: What is a pterygium of the conjunctiva and cornea?

options.text: A benign pink growth from conjunctiva onto cornea
options.explanation: Pterygium is a noncancerous conjunctival growth that extends onto the cornea.

options.text: A malignant tumor arising from the retina
options.explanation: Retinal tumors are not pterygia and do not grow from conjunctiva onto cornea.

options.text: An infectious ulcer of the corneal surface
options.explanation: Pterygium is not an infection or ulcer of the cornea.

options.text: A congenital opacity of the lens
options.explanation: Lens opacities describe cataracts, not conjunctival growths.

question: Which management approach is most appropriate for caudal regression syndrome?

options.text: Supportive care with surgery, rehabilitation, and management of associated anomalies
options.explanation: Caudal regression syndrome is managed symptomatically with surgery, rehabilitation, and treatment of associated problems such as bladder or orthopedic abnormalities.

options.text: High-dose antibiotics alone
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not treat this congenital malformation unless there is a separate infection.

options.text: Immediate chemotherapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy has no role in a structural congenital developmental disorder.

options.text: Radiation therapy to the spine
options.explanation: Radiation therapy is not a treatment for congenital spinal malformations.

question: What are craniofacial abnormalities?

options.text: Birth defects of the face or head
options.explanation: Craniofacial abnormalities are congenital defects involving the skull and face.

options.text: Inflammation of facial muscles after infection
options.explanation: This describes an inflammatory condition, not a congenital craniofacial defect.

options.text: Acquired fractures of the skull and jaw
options.explanation: Fractures are acquired injuries, not birth defects.

options.text: Degenerative disease of facial nerves in adults
options.explanation: This refers to nerve degeneration, not abnormalities of skull or facial development.

question: Which of the following can cause tarsal tunnel syndrome?

options.text: Repetitive stress, trauma, flat feet, or space-occupying lesions
options.explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is caused by compression from repetitive stress, trauma, flat feet, excess weight, or local space-occupying lesions.

options.text: Hyperthyroidism causing peripheral neuropathy
options.explanation: Hyperthyroidism can cause neuropathy, but it is not a typical cause of tarsal tunnel syndrome.

options.text: Femoral artery occlusion
options.explanation: Femoral artery occlusion affects limb blood flow, not compression of the tibial nerve in the tarsal tunnel.

options.text: Carpal bone fracture
options.explanation: Carpal bone fracture involves the wrist, not the ankle region where tarsal tunnel syndrome occurs.

question: What is the most likely cause of celiac artery compression syndrome?

options.text: Compression of the celiac artery by the median arcuate ligament
options.explanation: The syndrome is classically attributed to median arcuate ligament compression of the celiac artery.

options.text: Autoimmune vasculitis of the celiac trunk
options.explanation: Autoimmune vasculitis can cause mesenteric ischemia but is not the typical mechanism here.

options.text: Thrombosis of the superior mesenteric artery
options.explanation: Superior mesenteric artery thrombosis causes acute intestinal ischemia, not celiac artery compression syndrome.

options.text: Congenital absence of the celiac artery
options.explanation: The syndrome involves external compression, not absence of the celiac artery.

question: What type of research is being conducted for absence of the septum pellucidum?

options.text: NINDS studies normal brain development mechanisms
options.explanation: NINDS supports studies of normal brain development to understand and prevent developmental brain disorders.

options.text: Only surgical trials to replace the missing septum
options.explanation: There is no standard surgical trial to replace an absent septum pellucidum.

options.text: Antibiotic trials to prevent fetal brain infection
options.explanation: Absence of the septum pellucidum is not treated with antibiotic trials.

options.text: Radiation therapy trials for congenital brain anomalies
options.explanation: Radiation therapy is not used for congenital absence of the septum pellucidum.

question: What is a solitary kidney?

options.text: A kidney that is enlarged due to infection
options.explanation: An enlarged kidney from infection is not the definition of a solitary kidney.

options.text: The presence of only one functioning kidney
options.explanation: A solitary kidney means a person has only one functioning kidney.

options.text: A kidney located in the pelvis
options.explanation: A pelvic kidney describes an abnormal location, not absence of a second kidney.

options.text: Two kidneys fused together
options.explanation: Fused kidneys are a congenital anomaly but still involve two kidneys.

question: What is the preferred treatment for wandering spleen?

options.text: Laparoscopic splenectomy
options.explanation: Wandering spleen is usually treated with splenectomy because of the risk of infarction, hemorrhage, and portal hypertension.

options.text: Splenopexy
options.explanation: Splenopexy has a higher risk of recurrence and complications and is not the preferred treatment.

options.text: Observation only
options.explanation: Observation does not address the risk of serious vascular complications from a mobile spleen.

options.text: Antibiotic therapy
options.explanation: Antibiotics do not correct the anatomical problem or prevent splenic torsion.

question: What is a recognized cause or risk factor for Chilaiditi syndrome?

options.text: Chronic lung disease with diaphragmatic elevation
options.explanation: Chronic lung disease is a known risk factor for Chilaiditi syndrome due to altered diaphragmatic and hepatic positioning.

options.text: Acute pancreatitis causing pancreatic necrosis
options.explanation: Acute pancreatitis is not a typical cause of Chilaiditi syndrome.

options.text: Gallstones obstructing the common bile duct
options.explanation: Gallstones do not cause the colonic interposition seen in Chilaiditi syndrome.

options.text: Peptic ulcer disease with gastric outlet obstruction
options.explanation: Peptic ulcer disease is unrelated to the anatomic factors that predispose to Chilaiditi syndrome.

question: Who is at highest risk for acute perineal injury in males?

options.text: Men who have undergone perineal surgery
options.explanation: Perineal surgery is the most likely cause of acute perineal injury in men.

options.text: Children using playground equipment
options.explanation: Playground equipment is more associated with straddle injuries, not acute perineal surgery-related injury.

options.text: Military personnel in combat
options.explanation: Combat is more associated with impalement injuries, not the most likely acute perineal injury here.

options.text: Construction workers handling tools
options.explanation: Construction work can cause impalement injuries, but it is not the highest-risk group for acute perineal injury.

question: Which management approach is appropriate for isolated Pierre Robin sequence?

options.text: Airway support, feeding assistance, and cleft palate repair
options.explanation: Isolated Pierre Robin sequence is managed with supportive airway and feeding measures, often followed by surgical correction of cleft palate.

options.text: Immediate chemotherapy and radiation therapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy and radiation are not treatments for a congenital craniofacial sequence.

options.text: Long-term anticoagulation therapy
options.explanation: Anticoagulation is unrelated to the structural and airway problems in Pierre Robin sequence.

options.text: Insulin therapy and glucose monitoring
options.explanation: Insulin therapy treats diabetes, not congenital jaw and palate abnormalities.

question: What best describes tubal ligation?

options.text: Temporary hormonal contraception
options.explanation: Hormonal contraception is temporary and does not involve surgery.

options.text: Permanent surgical birth control
options.explanation: Tubal ligation is a permanent surgical method of birth control that closes the fallopian tubes.

options.text: Emergency contraception after intercourse
options.explanation: Emergency contraception is used after intercourse and is not a permanent procedure.

options.text: Treatment for infertility
options.explanation: Tubal ligation prevents pregnancy rather than treating infertility.

question: What is the most likely origin of a craniopharyngioma?

options.text: Epithelial remnants of Rathke's pouch
options.explanation: Craniopharyngiomas arise from embryologic epithelial remnants of Rathke's pouch or the craniopharyngeal duct.

options.text: Neural crest cells of the pituitary stalk
options.explanation: Neural crest cells do not give rise to craniopharyngiomas.

options.text: Meningothelial cells of the sella turcica
options.explanation: Meningothelial cells are associated with meningiomas, not craniopharyngiomas.

options.text: Astrocytes of the optic chiasm
options.explanation: Astrocytes are glial cells and are not the source of craniopharyngiomas.

question: Which treatment approach is most appropriate for Poland syndrome?

options.text: Observation only, as it always resolves spontaneously
options.explanation: Poland syndrome does not typically resolve spontaneously and may require intervention.

options.text: Surgical reconstruction with rehabilitation and supportive care
options.explanation: Poland syndrome is managed with reconstructive surgery and rehabilitation when needed to improve chest wall and limb function.

options.text: High-dose antibiotics and immobilization
options.explanation: Antibiotics and immobilization are not treatments for this congenital structural anomaly.

options.text: Chemotherapy followed by radiation therapy
options.explanation: Chemotherapy and radiation are used for malignancy, not congenital chest wall deformities.

question: What best describes talipes equinovarus?

options.text: Congenital inward and downward turning of the foot
options.explanation: Talipes equinovarus is a congenital deformity in which the foot turns inward and downward.

options.text: Acquired outward deviation of the foot after trauma
options.explanation: This describes an acquired deformity, not the congenital inward-downward position of talipes equinovarus.

options.text: Inflammation of the ankle joint causing swelling
options.explanation: Inflammation of the ankle joint is not the definition of talipes equinovarus.

options.text: Congenital absence of the foot bones
options.explanation: Talipes equinovarus does not involve absence of foot bones; it is a positional deformity.

question: Which finding best describes Poland syndrome?

options.text: Unilateral absent pectoralis major with ipsilateral hand webbing
options.explanation: Poland syndrome classically causes unilateral chest muscle hypoplasia or absence with ipsilateral syndactyly.

options.text: Bilateral cleft lip and palate with micrognathia
options.explanation: Cleft lip and palate with micrognathia describes a craniofacial anomaly, not Poland syndrome.

options.text: Progressive proximal muscle weakness with elevated CK
options.explanation: Progressive proximal weakness with elevated CK suggests a myopathy, not a congenital chest wall defect.

options.text: Absent radius with thumb hypoplasia
options.explanation: Absent radius with thumb hypoplasia is characteristic of radial ray defects, not Poland syndrome.

question: Which finding best describes antecubital pterygium?

options.text: Anterior elbow webbing with limited flexion
options.explanation: It incorrectly describes anterior webbing and flexion limitation, whereas flexion is usually unimpeded.

options.text: Antecubital webbing with posterior radial head subluxation
options.explanation: Antecubital pterygium features antecubital webbing and posterior subluxation of the radial head with limited extension.

options.text: Olecranon bursitis with elbow hyperextension
options.explanation: Olecranon bursitis does not cause the characteristic congenital webbing or radial head maldevelopment.

options.text: Cubital tunnel syndrome with ulnar nerve palsy
options.explanation: Cubital tunnel syndrome is an acquired nerve entrapment, not a congenital elbow webbing disorder.

question: What is an accessory navicular bone?

options.text: A small extra bone near the navicular on the inner midfoot
options.explanation: An accessory navicular is an extra bone adjacent to the navicular on the medial midfoot.

options.text: A fracture of the navicular bone after ankle trauma
options.explanation: This describes a fracture, not a normal accessory bone variant.

options.text: An accessory bone behind the heel near the Achilles tendon
options.explanation: This location corresponds to the os trigonum, not the accessory navicular.

options.text: A congenital fusion of the metatarsals in the forefoot
options.explanation: This describes a different congenital foot anomaly, not an accessory navicular.

question: Which finding is most consistent with an ectopic kidney?

options.text: Often asymptomatic and found incidentally
options.explanation: Ectopic kidneys are often asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on imaging or exam.

options.text: Severe flank pain in all patients
options.explanation: Severe flank pain is not universal and many patients have no pain at all.

options.text: Always causes gross hematuria
options.explanation: Gross hematuria is not a typical constant feature of ectopic kidney.

options.text: Always presents with fever and dysuria
options.explanation: Fever and dysuria suggest infection, not the usual presentation of ectopic kidney.

question: What is situs inversus?

options.text: Mirror-image reversal of internal organ arrangement
options.explanation: Situs inversus is a mirror-image arrangement of the internal organs compared with normal anatomy.

options.text: Complete absence of the spleen and liver
options.explanation: Asplenia with absent liver is not situs inversus and describes a different congenital anomaly.

options.text: Abnormal rotation of the intestines only
options.explanation: Abnormal intestinal rotation alone is malrotation, not a global mirror-image organ arrangement.

options.text: Congenital displacement of the heart alone
options.explanation: Heart displacement alone is dextrocardia, which may occur with or without situs inversus.

question: Which symptom is most characteristic of coccygodynia?

options.text: Pain when sitting on a hard chair
options.explanation: Coccygodynia classically causes pain with pressure on the tailbone, especially while sitting.

options.text: Painless swelling over the coccyx
options.explanation: Coccygodynia is painful, not typically a painless swelling.

options.text: Radiating leg weakness
options.explanation: Leg weakness suggests neurologic disease, not coccygodynia.

options.text: Fever with tailbone tenderness
options.explanation: Fever suggests infection rather than isolated coccyx pain.

question: Is empty sella syndrome typically inherited?

options.text: Yes, it is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern
options.explanation: There is no usual autosomal dominant inheritance pattern for empty sella syndrome.

options.text: Yes, it is always inherited from an affected parent
options.explanation: It is not always inherited from an affected parent, as most cases are sporadic.

options.text: No, it is typically not inherited
options.explanation: Empty sella syndrome is typically not inherited, though rare familial cases have been reported.

options.text: No, it is caused only by a mitochondrial mutation
options.explanation: A mitochondrial mutation is not the established cause of empty sella syndrome.

question: Which evaluation most commonly identifies situs inversus?

options.text: Physical exam with chest and abdominal imaging plus ECG
options.explanation: A thorough physical examination with chest and abdominal radiography and electrocardiography identifies most cases of situs inversus.

options.text: Serum amylase and lipase testing alone
options.explanation: Serum amylase and lipase may assess pancreatitis but do not diagnose organ reversal.

options.text: Colonoscopy with biopsy
options.explanation: Colonoscopy evaluates the colon lumen and does not identify situs inversus reliably.

options.text: Pulmonary function testing
options.explanation: Pulmonary function testing assesses lung mechanics, not visceral organ position.

question: Which imaging study is commonly used to confirm a suspected urachal cyst?

options.text: Ultrasound of the midline suprapubic region
options.explanation: Ultrasound is a first-line test to confirm a suspected urachal cyst.

options.text: Barium swallow study
options.explanation: A barium swallow evaluates the esophagus and upper gastrointestinal tract, not the urachus.

options.text: Electrocardiogram
options.explanation: An electrocardiogram assesses cardiac rhythm and cannot diagnose a urachal cyst.

options.text: Colonoscopy
options.explanation: Colonoscopy examines the colon and is not used to evaluate urachal remnants.

question: What is the cause of an accessory navicular bone?

options.text: Congenital fusion of the talus and calcaneus
options.explanation: Talocalcaneal fusion causes tarsal coalition, not an accessory navicular.

options.text: Unknown cause, sometimes linked to flatfoot or repeated sprains
options.explanation: The cause is unknown, though it may be associated with pes planus or repeated ankle sprains.

options.text: Acute infection of the navicular bone
options.explanation: Infection does not cause an accessory navicular bone.

options.text: Traumatic fracture of the medial cuneiform
options.explanation: A medial cuneiform fracture is unrelated to accessory navicular development.

question: Which description best defines an ectopic kidney?

options.text: A kidney located in an abnormal position
options.explanation: An ectopic kidney is a congenital kidney located below, above, or on the opposite side of its usual position.

options.text: A kidney with multiple cysts causing enlargement
options.explanation: Multiple cysts describe polycystic kidney disease, not ectopic kidney.

options.text: A kidney infected by bacteria in the pelvis
options.explanation: Bacterial infection of the kidney is pyelonephritis, not an ectopic location.

options.text: A kidney absent from both sides at birth
options.explanation: Absence of both kidneys is bilateral renal agenesis, not ectopic kidney.

question: What is the main purpose of knee replacement surgery?

options.text: To relieve pain and disability from arthritis while preserving movement
options.explanation: Knee replacement is primarily done to reduce pain and disability from arthritis or other joint disease while maintaining motion.

options.text: To treat acute knee infection with antibiotics
options.explanation: Antibiotics treat infection, but they do not describe the purpose of knee replacement surgery.

options.text: To repair torn menisci after sports injury
options.explanation: Meniscal tears are usually managed with repair or conservative treatment, not joint replacement.

options.text: To strengthen weak quadriceps muscles
options.explanation: Quadriceps strengthening is rehabilitation, not the indication for knee replacement.

question: What is coccygodynia?

options.text: Pain in and around the coccyx, often after direct trauma
options.explanation: Coccygodynia is pain in and around the coccyx, commonly following a fall or injury.

options.text: Inflammation of the sacroiliac joint causing low back stiffness
options.explanation: Sacroiliac joint inflammation is sacroiliitis, not coccygodynia.

options.text: Infection of the coccyx causing fever and drainage
options.explanation: Coccygodynia is not defined by coccygeal infection with fever or drainage.

options.text: Fracture of the lumbar vertebrae causing radicular pain
options.explanation: Lumbar vertebral fracture causes back pain, not isolated tailbone pain.

question: Approximately how common is coloboma in the general population?

options.text: 1 in 1,000 people
options.explanation: This is much more common than the reported prevalence of coloboma.

options.text: 1 in 10,000 people
options.explanation: Coloboma occurs in approximately 1 in 10,000 people.

options.text: 1 in 100,000 people
options.explanation: This is much rarer than the reported prevalence of coloboma.

options.text: 1 in 10 people
options.explanation: This prevalence is far too high for coloboma.

question: What is a thyroglossal duct cyst?

options.text: A congenital neck cyst from persistent embryologic thyroid tract tissue
options.explanation: It results from remnants of the thyroglossal duct left behind during thyroid development.

options.text: An infected lymph node in the anterior neck
options.explanation: An infected lymph node is not a congenital midline cyst from thyroid embryology.

options.text: A cyst arising from the parotid salivary gland
options.explanation: Parotid gland cysts arise in the salivary gland, not the thyroglossal tract.

options.text: A malignant tumor of the thyroid gland
options.explanation: A thyroglossal duct cyst is usually benign and not a thyroid malignancy.

question: What is the appropriate interpretation of absence of the septum pellucidum on imaging?

options.text: A standalone disorder requiring surgical correction
options.explanation: It is not itself a condition that requires surgical treatment.

options.text: A normal variant with no clinical significance
options.explanation: It is not always clinically insignificant because it can indicate an underlying developmental syndrome.

options.text: A characteristic finding seen in septo-optic dysplasia and other anomalies
options.explanation: Absence of the septum pellucidum alone is not a disorder; it is a finding associated with septo-optic dysplasia and other developmental anomalies.

options.text: A sign of acute intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: It is not a typical imaging sign of acute intracranial hemorrhage.

question: What does anatomy study?

options.text: The structure of the body
options.explanation: Anatomy is the science that studies the structure of the body.

options.text: The function of organs
options.explanation: Function of organs is studied in physiology, not anatomy.

options.text: The causes of disease
options.explanation: Causes of disease are studied in pathology, not anatomy.

options.text: The treatment of illness
options.explanation: Treatment of illness is the focus of clinical medicine, not anatomy.

question: What is the estimated prevalence of enlarged parietal foramina?

options.text: 1 in 1,500 to 5,000 individuals
options.explanation: This range is much more common than the reported prevalence.

options.text: 1 in 15,000 to 50,000 individuals
options.explanation: Enlarged parietal foramina are estimated to affect about 1 in 15,000 to 50,000 individuals.

options.text: 1 in 150,000 to 500,000 individuals
options.explanation: This range is far rarer than the estimated prevalence.

options.text: 1 in 100 to 1,000 individuals
options.explanation: This range is far too common for this rare condition.
